critique.summary: The article discusses the longstanding quadratic time complexity problem in regex engines when finding all matches, a problem that has persisted since the 1970s. It explains how common regex engines handle this inefficiently, introduces the RE# engine which solves the problem with a two-pass approach, and compares it to other algorithms like Aho-Corasick and Hyperscan.
mainPoints: Traditional regex engines suffer from quadratic time complexity when finding all matches, especially with certain patterns and large inputs.
Backtracking regex engines can have even worse exponential time complexity and remain widely used despite known issues.
The quadratic all-matches problem is distinct from single-match complexity and has been largely overlooked in academic research.
Aho-Corasick algorithm solves the linear-time all-matches problem but only for fixed strings, not full regexes.
Hyperscan achieves linear-time all-matches by using earliest-match semantics, which changes the match results and is unsuitable for some use cases.
RE# is a new regex engine that solves the quadratic all-matches problem for full regexes using a novel two-pass DFA approach without changing semantics.
RE# includes a hardened mode that guarantees linear time even on pathological inputs, at the cost of slower performance on typical inputs.
RE# currently lacks support for capture groups and lazy quantifiers, focusing on leftmost-longest semantics.
RE# shows competitive or superior performance compared to other engines on various benchmarks, especially on large pattern sets due to better cache behavior.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about the quadratic time complexity problem in regex engines when finding all matches are well-documented and supported by references to known research and benchmarks, including Russ Cox's work and BurntSushi's rebar benchmark suite. The explanation of backtracking issues and the distinction between single-match and all-matches complexity aligns with established knowledge in computer science. The description of Aho-Corasick's algorithm and Hyperscan's earliest-match semantics is accurate. The introduction of RE# and its two-pass approach is presented as novel but plausible, with benchmark data supporting performance claims. The article is transparent about limitations and tradeoffs, enhancing credibility. Overall, the information is consistent with external sources and technical literature.
considerations: The article does not provide independent third-party verification of RE#'s performance claims beyond internal benchmarks.
It does not discuss how RE# integrates with existing tools or ecosystems or its adoption status.
The impact of lacking capture groups and lazy quantifiers on practical usage scenarios is only briefly mentioned.
Security implications related to regex denial-of-service (ReDoS) attacks are touched on but could be expanded.
The article is authored by the developer of RE#, which may introduce bias toward emphasizing its advantages.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the complexity of finding all regex matches, particularly the quadratic time behavior in pathological cases. Commenters debate practical implications, theoretical edge cases, and possible mitigations like restricting regex features or sandboxing execution.

comments.author: zahlman
comments.text:
> search a document for a pattern and it takes a second. search one a hundred times larger and it doesn't take a hundred seconds - it can take almost three hours.

Most of this is about quadratic time find-all operations where a search operation is linear. But it's also still possible to get quadratic behaviour out of a single search without catastrophic backtracking, more easily than you might expect. In late January to early February, Tim Peters was talking about an example of this on the Python forums (see e.g. https://discuss.python.org/t/add-re-prefixmatch-deprecate-re...) and also related the experience of trying to diagnose the issue with AI (see https://discuss.python.org/t/claude-code-how-much-hype-how-m... and onward). Peters' example was:

  \d+\s+
on a string containing only digits, a prefix match takes O(n) time as it considers every possible end position for the digit, and immediately sees no following whitespace. But the search is quadratic because it has to repeat that O(n) work at every position; the regex engine can't track the fact that it's already examined the string and found no whitespace, so it re-tries each digit match length.

(This is arguably "backtracking" since it tries the longest match first, but clearly not in a catastrophic way; if you use `\d+?` instead then of course it only searches forward but is still O(n). It actually is slower in my testing in the Python implementation; I don't exactly know why. As noted in the discussion, the possessive quantifier `\d++` is considerably faster, and of course doesn't backtrack, but still causes O(n^2) searching. The repeated attempts to match `\s+` aren't the problem; the problem is repeatedly looking for digits in places where digits were already found and rejected.)

The way to fix this proposed in the discussion is to use a negative lookbehind assertion before the digits: `(?<!\d)\d+\s+`. This way, the regex engine can bail out early when it's in the middle of a digit string; if the previous character was a digit, then either `\d+\s+` doesn't match here, or it would have matched there.

A simpler idea is to just search for `\d\s+`, or even `\d\s` — since these will be present if and only if `\d+\s+` is. This way, though, you still need to do extra work with the partial match to identify the start and end of the full match. My first idea was to use positive lookbehind for the digits, since the lookbehind match doesn't need to backtrack. In fact lookbehinds require a fixed-length pattern, so this is really just a more complicated way to do the `\d\s+` simplification.

----

> Hyperscan (and its fork Vectorscan) is a true linear-time all-matches regex engine. it achieves this by using "earliest match" semantics - reporting a match the moment the DFA enters a match state, instead of continuing to find the longest one.

Is this not just equivalent to forcing "reluctant" quantifiers (`\d+?`) everywhere?
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, accurate explanation of how quadratic behavior arises even without catastrophic backtracking, supported by real examples and proposed fixes.

comments.author: 10000truths
comments.text:
Restricting regex features to guarantee time complexity works, but it requires sacrificing potentially useful features like backtracking (or in the article's case, constraining oneself to fixed-upper-bound-length needles).

In a real-world deployment where you want to run any arbitrary regex in an idiot/malice-proof manner, the best solution is the same solution you'd use for running any other kind of untrusted code - sandbox it! A good regex API should limit its execution time and memory consumption and return a timeout error in case those limits are exceeded. Ideally, those parameters would be configurable at the API level. Unfortunately, the only regex libraries I know of that get this right are .NET's standard library Regex API and the third-party regex package in Python.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a practical approach to handling arbitrary regex safely by sandboxing, which is a well-known and effective strategy in real-world systems.

comments.author: nine_k
comments.text:
> nearly everything that matters in practice: where the matches are, how long they are, and how many there are

I would say that regexes that matter in practice, e.g. when digging through logs, have clear boundaries that curb the pathological backtracking behavior. In particular, I find it difficult to imagine a practical need to find all matches of an expression like /.*a|b/, as shown in the article. Realistically you'd have to handle /\b.*a|b\b/, or similar, because realistically when you need all matches, you don't want intersecting matches. This means you want to proceed past the end of the n-th match to look for n+1-th match, and never want to use indeterminate prefixes like /.*a/.

This OTOH gives a reasonably useful heuristic if your regexp comes from an untrusted source and could be adversarial. Check that it does not start with a prefix with a Kleene star, like /a*/. Require at least one positive match (in each alternate branch). Of course, /a+b|c/ would still be quadratic if your text is long sequences of "a" interspersed with characters other than "b". But this, again, is more of a theoretical case, to my mind.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully distinguishes practical regex usage from theoretical worst cases, emphasizing heuristics that avoid pathological performance.

comments.author: ogogmad
comments.text:
Here's an NFA method that takes O(mn) time and (in the worst case) O(mn) memory:

Begin with the regex R. Form the NFA "A" of /.*R/. Run A over a string, marking the set of locations "MatchEnds" where any of A's states is accepting. Then form the NFA A' of the regex which matches the REVERSE of any string matched by R. Now run A' on the reversed string, but:

- A' should start in an EMPTY SET OF STATES

- A' should only go into its entry states every time it enters a location in MatchEnds.

- Furthermore, have each state of A' house a subset of MatchEnds, which gets ferried along any state transition.

- For each accepting state of A' and reversed string location i, make a record of which subset of MatchEnds is in the accepting state.
comments.analysis: This comment proposes a technical approach to improve regex matching efficiency, showing deep understanding though it is more complex and less accessible.

comments.author: thaumasiotes
comments.text:
> the problem we're talking about in this post (finding all longest matches without quadratic blowup)

Wait, what? I thought this was about finding all matches. With a minor tweak to the opening example:

We want to match `(.*a | b)` against `bbbbbabbbbb`.

I want to detect each `b` individually, and I also want to detect `bbbbba`, `bbbba`, `bbba`, `bba`, `ba`, and `a`. That's what it means to find all matches.
comments.analysis: This comment clarifies the scope of the problem discussed, emphasizing the difference between finding all matches versus longest matches, which is important for understanding the article.

comments.author: adzm
comments.text:
Is there any reason that RE#'s two-pass approach couldn't be adopted by other regex engines?

Ah, there is a post with more detail about RE# and discussion here recently that I must have missed: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47206647
comments.analysis: This comment raises a constructive question about applying a known technique from one regex engine to others, encouraging further exploration.

comments.author: gpvos
comments.text: I find it weird to have the Perl innovation (?:...) be called "traditional regex". Perl was rather innovative back then, even if it's more than 30 years ago now. Traditional regex is what came before it (grep -E being the most advanced form). I wonder what counts as nontraditional in the author's eyes.
comments.analysis: This comment provides historical context on regex terminology, correcting a potential misconception about what 'traditional regex' means.
